sections.paragraph:
Med. Yod, to boil up, to bubble up; compare سَاَر to spring up, to boil or bubble up as wine, anger, a fever, Hebr. שָׂאַר and שָׁאַר to ferment, גִּיר to boil up, to ferment, gähren. Hence-

סיר

comm. ( Jer 1:13 Eze 24:6).

(1) a pot, so called from boiling and bubbling, Jer 1:13 Eze 11:3, Eze 11:7 24:3 , Eze 24:6. סִיר הַבָּשָׂר Exo 16:3. Psa 60:10, מֹואָב סִיר רַחְצִי “Moab shall be the pot (or basin) of my washing,” my wash-pot; contemptuously said for, I will use it as the meanest vessel. Plur. סִירֹות Exo 38:3 1Ki 7:45.

(2) plur. סִירִים thorns, briers, so called from the idea of boiling or bubbling up, a notion which is applied to the redundant and luxuriant growth of plants (das Aufwuchern); especially in woods, see the root יָעַר and יַעַר No. 2 , Isa 34:13 Hos 2:8. Used with a paronomasia in this passage, Ecc 7:6, כְּקֹול הַסִּירִים תַּחַת הַסִּיר “like the crackling of (kindled) thorns under a pot.” A thicket is used poetically, as an image of impiety. Nah 1:10, עַד סִירִים סְבֻכִים “they are folded together as thorns,” (see עַד B, 2, ). Comp. Mic 7:4 Eze 2:6. It also denotes a hook, a fish hook, from its resemblance to a thorn (compare חֹוחַ). Plural סִירֹות Amo 4:2. (I formerly referred סִירִים thorns, to the root סוּר, so that it would properly signify recedanea, the degenerate parts of a shrub, compare סוּרֵי הַנֶּפֶן Jer 2:21 but it is preferable to refer the word סִיר in both significations to the same origin).
